name: anubis (by osiris
shortDescription: Anubis is the ancient Egyptian god of embalming and the dead, guiding souls into the afterlife. He is often depicted as a jackal-headed figure or as a wild dog.
description: Anubis, or Inpu in ancient Egyptian, held a vital role in the funerary rites and the journey of souls into the afterlife. He was primarily responsible for embalming the dead, ensuring their bodies were preserved for the afterlife. His jackal head is believed to be associated with the wild dogs that frequented cemeteries, scavenging for remains, though it also may symbolize protection. According to some myths, Anubis was the illegitimate son of Nephthys and Osiris, conceived when Nephthys tricked Osiris into sleeping with her by disguising herself as his wife, Isis. Fearing Set's wrath, Nephthys abandoned Anubis, who was then found and raised by Isis. Anubis is credited with inventing the process of mummification to preserve Osiris' body after Set murdered him. Anubis played a crucial role in the Weighing of the Heart ceremony, where the deceased's heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice). If the heart was lighter than the feather, the soul could proceed to the afterlife; if heavier, it would be devoured by the demon Ammit. He guided souls through the underworld, protecting them from dangers and ensuring their safe passage. Anubis's significance lessened somewhat with the rise of Osiris as the primary god of the underworld, but he remained an important figure in funerary rituals and beliefs.
epithets: He Who is Upon His Mountain

sources.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.author: Various Scribes

sources.title: Religion and Magic in Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Rosalie David

worshipPractices.name: Offerings at Tombs
worshipPractices.description: Food, drink, and other valuable items were left at tombs as offerings to Anubis. This was done to appease him and ensure his protection and guidance for the deceased in the afterlife. Priests dedicated to Anubis also performed rituals and incantations.

worshipPractices.name: Embalming and Mummification Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Anubis was central to the embalming process, with priests wearing jackal masks to represent him. They performed the complex rituals of removing organs, drying the body, and wrapping it in linen, all while reciting spells to ensure the deceased's safe passage.

festivals.name: Festival of Khoiak
festivals.timing: During the fourth month of the Egyptian calendar (Khoiak)
festivals.description: While dedicated primarily to Osiris, Anubis played a key role in the Khoiak festival. The festival involved rituals surrounding death and rebirth, with Anubis guiding the soul of Osiris (and all the dead) through the underworld and towards resurrection.

cultural.modernLegacy: Anubis remains a powerful symbol of ancient Egypt, often featured in books, movies, and video games. His image is frequently used to represent themes of death, the afterlife, and mystery, contributing to the ongoing fascination with Egyptian mythology. He also influences modern art and design, inspiring unique interpretations of his iconic jackal-headed form.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Hermes Psychopompos guides newly deceased souls to the underworld, similar to Anubis's role in guiding souls through the afterlife. Both deities facilitate the transition from the world of the living to the realm of the dead.

Xolotl, often depicted as a dog-headed deity, guides the dead on their journey to Mictlan (the underworld). Like Anubis, Xolotl is associated with both death and the transition to the afterlife, acting as a psychopomp.

The Ankou is a figure in Celtic mythology who collects the souls of the dead. While not directly involved in embalming, the Ankou's duty is to ensure the deceased are transported to the afterlife, a task akin to Anubis's role in the Egyptian funerary process.

Yama is the Hindu god of death who judges the dead and presides over the underworld. Though not jackal-headed, Yama is the primary deity associated with death and the afterlife, similar to Anubis's prominent role in the Egyptian funerary beliefs.

associations.name: Jackal
associations.significance: Anubis' jackal head is linked to the canids that frequented cemeteries, scavenging and protecting the dead.

associations.name: Black
associations.significance: Black symbolizes both the fertile soil of the Nile and the decomposition of the body, representing rebirth and transformation in the afterlife.

associations.name: Judgment
associations.significance: Anubis oversaw the weighing of the heart ceremony, determining the worthiness of the deceased to enter the afterlife.

associations.name: Desert Sand
associations.significance: The desert where cemeteries were often located, signifying the transition to the afterlife and Anubis's domain.

associations.name: Protection
associations.significance: Anubis protected the dead from desecration and guided them through the dangers of the underworld.
Associated with the four sons of Horus, protectors of the canopic jars which held the organs, linking to Anubis' funerary role.

associations.name: Sirius
associations.significance: The rising of Sirius was associated with the Nile's flood and rebirth, potentially linking to Anubis' role in afterlife transition.

companions.name: Thoth
companions.description: Thoth, the god of wisdom and writing, often worked alongside Anubis in the Hall of Ma'at during the Weighing of the Heart ceremony, recording the results. He helped ensure the deceased's judgment was accurately recorded.

companions.name: Ma'at
companions.description: Ma'at, the goddess of truth and justice, was crucial to Anubis's role in the afterlife. The Feather of Ma'at was used to weigh against the heart of the deceased, determining their worthiness to enter the afterlife.

companions.name: Imsety
companions.description: Imsety, one of the four sons of Horus, protected the liver of the deceased within a canopic jar. Anubis oversaw the proper preparation and protection of these vital organs.

companions.name: Hapi
companions.description: Hapi, one of the four sons of Horus, protected the lungs of the deceased within a canopic jar. Anubis oversaw the proper preparation and protection of these vital organs.

companions.name: Duamutef
companions.description: Duamutef, one of the four sons of Horus, protected the stomach of the deceased within a canopic jar. Anubis oversaw the proper preparation and protection of these vital organs.

companions.name: Qebehsenuef
companions.description: Qebehsenuef, one of the four sons of Horus, protected the intestines of the deceased within a canopic jar. Anubis oversaw the proper preparation and protection of these vital organs.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The parentage of Anubis is complex and varies across different periods of Egyptian history. In early mythology, Anubis was believed to be the son of Ra and Hesat, a cow goddess associated with nourishment and fertility. This connection highlights Anubis's early association with life and death cycles, as Hesat provided sustenance while Anubis guided souls through their transition. Later myths, particularly from the Old Kingdom onward, shifted his parentage, often depicting him as the offspring of Nephthys and Osiris. This revised genealogy is crucial, as it explains Anubis's central role in the Osiris myth. Nephthys, wife of Set, secretly conceived Anubis with Osiris, her brother-in-law. Fearing Set's wrath, Nephthys abandoned the infant Anubis, who was then found and raised by Isis, Osiris's wife. This adoption by Isis further solidified Anubis's connection to resurrection and protection, as Isis was renowned for her magical abilities and devotion to her husband. The shifting narrative surrounding Anubis's birth reflects the evolving theological landscape of ancient Egypt and the gradual integration of local deities into a more unified pantheon. The association with both Ra and Osiris underscores his importance as a deity who bridged the terrestrial and celestial realms.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Temples
extendedContent.content: While Anubis did not have large, dedicated temples comparable to those of Ra or Amun, his presence was ubiquitous throughout Egypt, particularly in areas associated with cemeteries and the necropolis. Priests dedicated to Anubis played a crucial role in the embalming process, wearing jackal masks to embody the god during funerary rites. The primary center of Anubis's cult was likely in Cynopolis ('City of the Dog') in Upper Egypt, although archaeological evidence is scarce. Numerous smaller shrines and chapels dedicated to Anubis have been discovered in Saqqara and other burial sites, reflecting his intimate connection with the deceased. Offerings to Anubis typically included food, drink, and incense, intended to appease the god and ensure safe passage for the deceased into the afterlife. Votive offerings, such as small statues of jackals or amulets depicting Anubis, were also common. The pervasive presence of Anubis in funerary art and religious texts, such as the Book of the Dead, demonstrates the widespread veneration he received from all levels of Egyptian society. His role was considered essential for a successful transition into the afterlife, making him a vital figure in the religious beliefs of the ancient Egyptians.

extendedContent.title: Iconography & Representation
extendedContent.content: Anubis is most recognizably depicted as a jackal-headed human or a full jackal, typically with black fur. The choice of black is not necessarily related to the color of jackals in Egypt, which are more commonly brown, but rather symbolizes the fertile black soil of the Nile Valley, representing regeneration and rebirth. This connection to the earth further emphasizes Anubis's role in the funerary process and the hope for resurrection. In his anthropomorphic form, Anubis is often portrayed wearing a kilt and holding the ankh (symbol of life) and the was-scepter (symbol of power). He is frequently depicted overseeing the weighing of the heart ceremony, a crucial judgment of the deceased's worthiness to enter the afterlife. During embalming rituals, priests wearing Anubis masks would perform the necessary procedures, symbolically embodying the god and ensuring the proper preservation of the body. These masks, often crafted from wood or leather, served as powerful reminders of Anubis's presence and his role in safeguarding the deceased. The iconography of Anubis consistently reinforces his association with death, protection, and the hope for eternal life, solidifying his position as a central figure in Egyptian funerary beliefs.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content: While Anubis's primary domain was the realm of death and the afterlife, his role extended to the broader cosmic order. He was not merely a guardian of the dead but also a preserver of cosmic balance, ensuring the proper transition of souls into the next life. His involvement in the weighing of the heart ceremony, as described in the Book of the Dead, demonstrates his role in upholding Ma'at, the Egyptian concept of truth, justice, and cosmic order. By weighing the deceased's heart against the feather of Ma'at, Anubis determined their worthiness to enter the afterlife. If the heart was lighter than the feather, the soul was deemed worthy and allowed to proceed to the realm of Osiris. If the heart was heavier, it was devoured by Ammit, the Devourer of Souls, resulting in eternal non-existence. This judgment highlights Anubis's responsibility in maintaining the cosmic order by ensuring that only those who lived a virtuous life were granted access to the afterlife. Furthermore, Anubis's association with mummification and the preservation of the body can be seen as an act of maintaining cosmic balance, preventing decay and chaos from encroaching upon the ordered world. His actions helped facilitate the soul's journey and uphold the principles of Ma'at within the realm of death.
